one_line_takeaway: PlanGuard defends tool-using LLM agents from indirect prompt injection by having a context-isolated planner define the allowed actions up front, then hard-rule + LLM-intent verifying every tool call against it — driving InjecAgent ASR to a structural 0% while cutting the naive rule-only false-positive rate from ~27-38% to ~1-3%.
key_finding: On InjecAgent, both the Stage-I-only variant and full PlanGuard drove ASR to exactly 0.0% on both the Direct Harm and Data Stealing subsets (versus an undefended Vanilla Agent ASR of 56.90% on DH and 88.67% on DS under the compliance prompt). The zero ASR is described as structural, not empirical luck: because the Isolated Planner never sees the poisoned context, injected instructions cannot enter Sref. The Stage II Intent Verifier is what makes the defense usable: Stage-I-only hard matching alone incurred a high FPR (27.00% DH, 38.01% DS), which the full system reduced to 0.97% (DH) and 3.28% (DS) by recovering benign but syntactically mismatched actions. The abstract reports the aggregate figures across the full dataset as ASR 72.8% → 0% and FPR 1.49%.
core_contribution: PlanGuard, a training-free, model-agnostic defense framework that protects tool-using LLM agents against Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) by enforcing 'Context Isolation' at the execution layer. Its two novel components are (1) an Isolated Planner P that, given only the trusted user instruction I and the tool definitions T (never the retrieved external context), produces a clean Reference Action Set Sref that bounds the tools and parameters the agent is allowed to use; and (2) a Hierarchical Verification Mechanism that intercepts every tool call the agent tries to execute and checks it against Sref. Stage I applies deterministic hard rules (exact string matching) and Stage II applies an LLM-based Tool Intent Verifier to distinguish benign formatting drift from malicious hijacking. The stated insight is that because the planner is architecturally cut off from the poisoned context, no injected payload can enter the reference set, so the defense is deterministic rather than probabilistic.
threat_model: Indirect Prompt Injection against tool-using LLM agents, scoped specifically to 'Actionable IPI' (attacks that trigger unauthorized tool executions, not toxic-text or misinformation generation). The user instruction I is trusted and the absolute root of trust; the attacker CANNOT modify I. Instead the adversary injects a malicious payload p_adv into untrusted external context C (e.g., emails, web pages, retrieved documents). Because current LLMs suffer 'Context Mixing' and cannot separate instructions from data, the agent perceives a composite instruction set I_effective = I ∪ p_adv. Two attack goals: Type I Unauthorized Tool Invocation / function hijacking (agent calls a tool t_adv unrelated to I, e.g. user asks 'Summarize this email' but agent runs SendEmail()); Type II Intent Deviation / argument hijacking (agent calls the correct tool but with malicious parameters, e.g. 'Delete the temporary folder' becomes DeleteFile('/system/root')). An attack succeeds if a_adv appears in the executed action sequence. The paper also considers a white-box adaptive attacker who knows the PlanGuard architecture.
limitations: 1) Context-Dependent Argument Hijacking is explicitly acknowledged as unsolved: because the Planner is isolated from external context (information asymmetry), when an instruction depends on implicit external information (e.g., 'Pay the bill in the email') the Planner can verify the action TYPE ('Pay') but has no ground truth for the specific argument values, leaving a gap the authors defer to future rule-based information-extraction work. 2) Computational/latency overhead: the framework adds two extra LLM inferences (planner + Stage II verifier) per request over a vanilla agent; the authors propose future SLM specialization to reduce cost. 3) The Stage II verifier is itself an LLM and thus a probabilistic component; the paper argues Stage I is the deterministic 'cornerstone' but Stage II's judgments remain model-dependent. 4) Evaluation is single-benchmark (InjecAgent only) and single-backbone (DeepSeek-V3.2 for both agent and defense), and depends on an artificially injected compliance directive to elicit vulnerability, so generalization to other agents/models is asserted ('model-agnostic') rather than demonstrated across models. 5) Adaptive-attack robustness (parameter-injection to fool Stage II) is argued theoretically via generation and schema-validation hurdles rather than tested empirically.

datasets_used: InjecAgent benchmark (1,054 test cases; 17 user tools; 62 attacker tools; two subsets: Direct Harm (DH) and Data Stealing (DS))
quantitative_results: Vanilla Agent ASR: 56.90% on Direct Harm subset
Vanilla Agent ASR: 88.67% on Data Stealing subset
Stage-I Only (Single Rule) ASR: 0.0% on both DH and DS
Full PlanGuard ASR: 0.0% on both DH and DS
Stage-I Only FPR: 27.00% on DH, 38.01% on DS
Full PlanGuard FPR: 0.97% on DH, 3.28% on DS
Aggregate figures reported in abstract: ASR reduced from 72.8% to 0%; FPR 1.49%
baselines_compared: Vanilla Agent (no defense, run under the compliance-inducing prompt)
Stage-I Only / Single Rule (ablation: planner hard-constraint matching without the Stage II Intent Verifier)
